question_title: Will the United States and Iran publicly agree to a formal ceasefire that both sides acknowledge before May 1, 2026?
summary: The question requires a FORMAL ceasefire with INDEFINITE duration publicly acknowledged by both the US and Iran before May 1, 2026. The current situation strongly points to NO: (1) The only existing ceasefire is a temporary two-week arrangement starting April 8, which explicitly does not qualify per the fine print; (2) Marathon 21-hour peace talks in Pakistan on April 11 ended in complete failure, with deep divisions on nuclear enrichment, the Strait of Hormuz, and Lebanon; (3) The temporary ceasefire expires around April 22-23, leaving at most ~7 days before the May 1 deadline; (4) The ceasefire is already being violated by both sides; (5) Both sides adopted confrontational postures after the failed talks. No new negotiations are scheduled, and the fundamental disagreements are far too deep to resolve in the remaining timeframe. A formal, indefinite ceasefire would require a dramatic and unprecedented breakthrough that all evidence argues against.

factors.description: Existing ceasefire is explicitly temporary (two weeks), which the fine print states does not count - only an indefinite-duration formal ceasefire qualifies
Marathon 21-hour peace talks in Islamabad on April 11 ended without any deal - deep divisions remain on nuclear program, Strait of Hormuz, and Lebanon
Two-week ceasefire expires around April 22-23, leaving only ~7 days before May 1 deadline even if extended, and no framework for a permanent deal exists
Ceasefire already being violated by both sides; Iran accuses US of violations, Israeli strikes continuing in Lebanon
Trump's dismissive stance ('Whether we make a deal or not makes no difference to me') and Vance's confrontational tone suggest US not prioritizing a permanent deal
Iran's chief negotiator blamed US for excessive demands; fundamental disagreements on enrichment, Hormuz tolls, and proxy groups remain
Diplomatic channels remain technically open - Iran's spokesman said 'diplomacy never ends' and Pakistan mediator characterized talks as 'neither breakthrough nor breakdown'
Status quo persistence: wartime formal peace agreements with indefinite duration are extremely rare to achieve within weeks, especially after failed initial talks

reasoning:
**Forecast: 3% probability (NO)**

**Key findings:**

- A **two-week temporary ceasefire** was agreed on April 7-8, 2026, mediated by Pakistan. However, the fine print explicitly excludes temporary ceasefires — only a **formal ceasefire with indefinite intended duration** counts.
- **Marathon 21-hour peace talks failed** on April 11 in Islamabad. VP Vance said Iran "chose not to accept our terms." Core disagreements persist on nuclear enrichment, Strait of Hormuz control/tolls, and Lebanon.
- The temporary ceasefire **expires ~April 22-23**, leaving at most a week before the May 1 deadline. No new talks are scheduled.
- The ceasefire is **already being violated by both sides**, and Trump adopted a dismissive posture ("Whether we make a deal or not makes no difference to me").
- Achieving a formal, indefinite-duration ceasefire acknowledged by both sides within ~17 days — after the most comprehensive negotiation attempt just collapsed — would require an unprecedented and implausible breakthrough.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the United States and Iran publicly agree to a formal ceasefire that both sides acknowledge before May 1, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43136)
[U.S. and Iran Fail To Reach Deal on Ending War After Marathon Talks](https://time.com/article/2026/04/11/strait-of-hormuz-iran-peace-talks/)
[US-Iran ceasefire talks: What are the key sticking points?](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/12/us-iran-ceasefire-talks-what-are-the-key-sticking-points)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if, before May 1, 2026, both the United States and Iran publicly acknowledge a formal ceasefire or equivalent bilateral halt to hostilities, using official statements from the White House at https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/ and the U.N. Security Council site at https://www.un.org/securitycouncil/ or other credible sources per https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions. Otherwise, this question resolves to no.
fine_print: An agreed-upon ceasefire must have an indefinite intended duration in order to count. Whether the ceasefire is complied with is immaterial. Temporary ceasefires, pauses, proposed ceasefire plans, or unconfirmed mediation reports do not count unless both sides publicly acknowledge a formal ceasefire. Candidate Sources: Reuters Middle East coverage https://www.reuters.com/world/middle-east/
